Broadly, reported rates range from 3% to 46%. Several studies cite recurrence between 5% and 20%, while others report 15% to 25%. One center reported a recurrence rate of 27.4% after a median follow-up of 38 months, with lower rates in microadenomas (19%) versus macroadenomas (45%). Protective factors against recurrence include lower immediate post-operative cortisol concentrations and longer duration of glucocorticoid replacement. These findings underscore the need for lifelong monitoring in all patients with Cushing's disease.

The 1 mg DST assesses the normal feedback reaction of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis; a morning serum cortisol below 50 nmol/L can exclude Cushing's Syndrome. The choice of test should be individualized based on patient characteristics and lifestyle.

69a8012b85870e396d000034.ideal_answer: The pathogenic prion protein (PrPSc) is formed from the normal cellular prion protein (PrPC) through a conformational conversion process. In the favored model, existing PrPSc acts as a template, directly interacting with endogenous PrPC and inducing its misfolding into nascent infectious prions. This templating mechanism is supported by the observation that antibodies binding cell-surface PrPC can inhibit PrPSc formation in a dose-dependent manner, effectively blocking the interaction between the two conformers. Trace neurometals such as copper, zinc, iron, and manganese, which normally bind to PrPC, are critical determinants of this conformational change and can influence PrPC toxicity. Misfolding of PrPC can also be induced at the plasma membrane by agents such as suramin or copper, triggering internalization and intracellular degradation. At the molecular level, specific point mutations can promote or resist conversion: the pathogenic E200K mutation disrupts crucial salt-bridge interactions and increases random motions between helices, facilitating conversion to PrPSc, whereas the E219K mutation enhances stability through stronger salt bridges and increased native contacts. Once formed, PrPSc aggregates accumulate in the brain, and cellular quality control mechanisms involving proteins such as p62 attempt to mediate their clearance through autophagy and proteasomal pathways.

69a7491c85870e396d00001c.ideal_answer: Cancer cells employ multiple sophisticated strategies to attack and disable immune cells, particularly CD8+ T cells, thereby promoting immune escape. They can upregulate signaling molecules such as ZEB1, which drives M2 polarization of tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) via the MCSF-CCL18 axis, leading to CD8+ T-cell apoptosis. Cancer cells also overexpress PD-L1, which binds PD-1 on T cells to induce T-cell apoptosis, anergy, and exhaustion. Proteins like SPOCK1 in lung cancer and galectin 4 in pancreatic cancer further suppress T-cell viability and infiltration. In hepatocellular carcinoma, cathepsin E (CTSE) promotes release of des-γ-carboxy prothrombin (DCP), which induces reactive oxygen species production and T-cell apoptosis. Cancer cells also release extracellular vesicles carrying microRNAs such as miR-24-3p, which impair T-cell signaling pathways and promote regulatory T-cell (Treg) development. Additionally, soluble factors in the tumor microenvironment, including IL-4, IL-10, and TGF-β, foster Treg differentiation and T-cell exhaustion. Collectively, these mechanisms allow cancer cells to neutralize cytotoxic immune responses, creating an immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment that facilitates tumor growth and metastasis.
